title: The Nations This Morning
headline: Ottawa named a port expansion as a national priority; Tsawwassen First Nation answered by naming the conditions.
throughLine: The weekend did not produce a flood of new Indigenous economic files. It produced one hard test: Roberts Bank Terminal 2 is now being treated as a national export-capacity file, while Tsawwassen First Nation is reminding governments and proponents that consent, compensation, employment and the environmental record still sit in front of the economics.
oneThing: Roberts Bank enters the fast-track conversation, with Tsawwassen setting conditions

title: The Nations This Morning
headline: Yesterday's national argument over consent now has a B.C. project file attached to it.
throughLine: The economic signal moved from Ottawa's AFN floor to B.C.'s project-review machinery. Chiefs spent the week saying national advocacy cannot replace affected First Nations' authority; one day later, B.C. and Simpcw signed a consent-based assessment agreement for the proposed Yellowhead copper mine.
oneThing: B.C. and Simpcw turn consent into the operating question for Yellowhead

title: The Nations This Morning
headline: The live economic question is not whether major projects move. It is who has authority to say yes, no, or not yet.
throughLine: The strongest signal in the past 24 hours came from Ottawa, where First Nations chiefs narrowed the Assembly of First Nations' role on major projects to national advocacy and insisted that Canada deal directly with affected rights and title holders. That turns the federal major-projects push from a permitting story into a governance and capital-allocation story.
oneThing: Chiefs draw a boundary around the AFN's role in major-project talks
